exemplar.title: Does the type of match played in badminton affect the reaction time of the athlete?

detailedExemplarData.title: Does the type of match played in badminton affect the reaction time of the athlete?

subject.title: Biology
subject.description:
**IB Biology** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **four interconnected themes: Unity and Diversity, Form and Function, Interaction and Interdependence, and Continuity and Change**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, scientific inquiry, data analysis, and practical investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice and data-based questions | 1 hour 20 min | 2 hours | 36% |
| **Paper 2** | Short and extended response questions | 1 hour 30 min | 2 hours 30 min | 44% |
| **Scientific Investigation** | Individual research project | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear, specific sporting context (nationalâlevel badminton tournament) that grounds the research question
Identification and discussion of multiple methodological considerations (controls vs. uncontrollable factors)
Step-by-step procedure with equipment list allowing reproduction with minor ambiguities
weaknesses: Discussion of sample size, randomization and statistical power is underdeveloped
Informal tone in places; literature review lacks critical appraisal of cited studies
Key details (e.g., lighting conditions, laptop latency calibration) are omitted
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand scores of 5 (context), 4 (considerations) and 4 (methodology description) yield an overall band in the 3â4 range, justifying a score of 4. The context is specific and wellâjustified, but methodological explanations and procedural details fall short of the âexplainedâ level needed for a 5.

strengths: Data are communicated through well-labelled tables and a bar graph with error bars and units
Means, standard deviations and coefficient of variation are correctly calculated and displayed
Error bars and individual values are highlighted clearly, supporting precise interpretation
weaknesses: Origin and propagation of the Â±0.5 ms uncertainty is not justified or applied in calculations
Inferential statistics (e.g., chi-square or appropriate ANOVA/t-test) are omitted
Some formatting inconsistencies (units in headers, missing legend for color coding) detract from clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments of 5 (communication), 3 (uncertainties) and 3 (processing) average to the mid-band (3â4). While presentation is precise, key analytical elements (uncertainty propagation, statistical testing) are missing, placing the work at a solid 4.

strengths: Conclusion directly addresses the research question and is consistent with descriptive results
Relevant comparisons to published studies are made, showing awareness of the scientific context
weaknesses: No inferential statistics presented (p-values, test details) to substantiate claims of non-significance
Comparisons to literature are superficial and lack critical appraisal of methodological differences
Restates descriptive statistics without confidence intervals or formal justification
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands are assessed at a mid-band level (3â4) but lack full consistency (no p-values) and depth in contextual comparison. The evidence supports a score of 3 in the conclusion criterion.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses are identified and their impacts on reliability/validity discussed
Realistic improvements are proposed that link directly to the identified limitations
Awareness of test familiarity and equipment standardization demonstrates thoughtful critique
weaknesses: Explanations of improvements vary in depth and lack detailed protocols (e.g., rest durations, monitoring procedures)
Relative impact of identified limitations is noted but not quantitatively prioritized
Feasibility analysis of suggested improvements is limited
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: With both strands in the upper half of band 3â4 (score 4), the evaluation demonstrates specific insights and linked improvements yet falls short of fully explaining and quantifying the impacts required for a 5â6 level.

strandSelections.reasoning: The conclusion directly answers the research question (no significant effect) and is generally **consistent with the presented descriptive analysis** (overlapping error bars). Nonetheless, because no statistical test is performed, the consistency cannot be fully demonstrated, so the justification is only partial. This aligns with the mid-band (3-4), score 3.

Data are communicated through **well-labelled tables and a bar graph with error bars and units (ms)**. Mean, standard deviation and coefficient of variation are clearly presented, and the fastest individual values are highlighted. Although the first tableâs formatting is slightly irregular, the information remains both clear and precise, fulfilling the 5-6 descriptor. A modest formatting issue prevents awarding the top mark of 6.

Relevant processing (means, SD, CV) is performed **accurately** and sample calculations are shown, but the promised chi-square significance test is not executed, and no inferential p-value is provided. This constitutes a significant omission for answering the research question. Therefore, the processing is adequate yet incomplete, matching the 3-4 band (score 3).

Specific weaknesses are described (test familiarity, anxiety, noise, gender mix, sample homogeneity) and the student **commentates on their potential impact** on reliability and validity (e.g., trembling hands compromising data). Explanations are present but not deeply prioritised or quantified, so the work reaches the upper half of the 3-4 band.

The student relates findings to several published studies on reaction time in badminton and other sports, noting similarities and differences. These comparisons are **relevant but somewhat superficial**, lacking a detailed critical appraisal of why results differ. Hence the strand meets the 3-4 descriptor, meriting a 3.

The research question is stated clearly and is embedded in a **specific sporting context** (national-level badminton tournament; singles, mixed-doubles and same-sex doubles). The introduction links the question to existing literature and to observable features of each match type, demonstrating why the investigation is worth pursuing. Because the context is concrete, authentic and appropriate to the biological topic of human reaction time in sport, the descriptor for the 5-6 band is met. Minor informal wording prevents a perfect 6, but the standard comfortably exceeds the 3-4 level.

The student quotes an instrumental uncertainty (Â±0.5 ms) and displays error bars based on standard deviation, demonstrating **some consideration of uncertainty**. However, the origin of the Â±0.5 ms value is not justified, website latency is only mentioned qualitatively, and propagation of uncertainties is absent. Consequently, omissions and possible inaccuracies remain, placing the work in the 3-4 band but closer to the lower end (score 3).

Several realistic improvements are linked to identified weaknesses (use a more demanding test, standardised equipment, wait until athletes calm down, separate by gender, recruit from multiple teams). The student **explains how these changes could enhance accuracy or variation**, but depth of explanation varies and feasibility is not always analysed. This satisfies the 3-4 band at the higher end (score 4).

The student **identifies and discusses several methodological considerations**: uncontrollable factors (mental state, order of games), controlled factors (noise, age range, skill level, injuries) and justification for using 10âmin post-match testing and five trials per condition. These considerations are relevant and generally sufficient to answer the question, but the discussion lacks depth on sample size, randomisation and statistical power, so the explanation is not fully elaborated. Hence the work fulfils the 3-4 band rather than the more rigorous âexplainedâ level of 5-6.

A clear step-by-step procedure, equipment list and control-variable table are provided; the number of athletes (10) and the reaction-time website are stated, allowing another investigator to repeat the study **with only minor ambiguities** (e.g. exact order of match types, environmental lighting, details of laptop latency). Because reproduction is feasible but not entirely unambiguous, the description satisfies the 3-4 band. It does not merit 5-6 because critical details such as chi-square set-up, exact timing of the five trials, and calibration of the 0.5 ms uncertainty are missing.

annotations.comment: The opening anecdote provides personal context but reads informally; consider framing this within existing literature to strengthen academic tone and situate the research question more objectively.
The literature review lists relevant studies but lacks critical appraisal of their methodologies and limitations; deepen the analysis by evaluating how each studyâs design informs your own.
The choice of the national badminton tournament provides a clear, authentic and specific context for investigating reaction time differences.
The informationâprocessing model is introduced but not explicitly linked to your experimental variables; explain how each stage relates to your simple reaction test design.
The list of factors affecting reaction time is comprehensive but lacks discussion of how you will prioritize or quantitatively measure each influence.
Discussion of uncontrollable factors (e.g., mental state, order effects) is descriptive but would benefit from indicating how these will be tracked or statistically accounted for.
The procedural list outlines key steps but omits details on athlete selection criteria, randomization of match order, and environmental controls (e.g., lighting levels).
Equipment list is clear but the noiseâreduction capacity of the earplugs should be specified (e.g., decibel attenuation rating) to demonstrate effective noise control.
Controlâvariable table lists noise qualitatively (âleast possibleâ) without quantitative benchmarks; include target decibel levels and confirm with a sound meter.
Age boundaries are justified by general trends but lack citation of the specific ageâreaction time relationship; include empirical data or references to support these cut-offs.
A chiâsquared test is intended for categorical data; reaction times are continuous. Consider using ANOVA or paired t-tests to assess differences between match types.
Ethical considerations note earplug waste but omit participant consent procedures and institutional approval; ensure informed consent and ethics review are documented.
Table 1 skeleton appears without data entries; remove placeholder content or populate fully to communicate recorded observations.
The reaction time results table is wellâstructured with units and error margins clearly indicated, facilitating precise interpretation.
Highlighting the fastest reaction times in green aids visual analysis but requires a legend or note in the caption to clarify the color coding.
Table 2 presents averages clearly but omits explicit units in the header; include âmsâ next to each mean to avoid ambiguity.
Figure 1 caption lacks detail on axis labels and error bar definitions; enhance the caption to specify axes, units and the source of uncertainty.
Standard deviation calculations are correctly displayed with formulas; this clarity supports reproducibility of your data processing.
Coefficient of variation is calculated correctly but should be expressed as a percentage and accompanied by interpretation regarding relative variability.
Summary of results restates descriptive statistics but omits inferential outcomes; include p-values or confidence intervals to justify your conclusion of no significant effect.
Evaluation section introduces potential limitations but lacks discussion of their relative impact on reliability and validity; quantify which factors most influence your results.
Identifying test familiarity as a limitation demonstrates awareness of methodological weaknesses and its specific impact on reaction times.
Acknowledging the 30 ms website lag is important but you should propagate this uncertainty through your mean and SD calculations to quantify its effect.
The conclusion cites statistical analysis but does not present test type, degrees of freedom or p-value; include these details to validate your claim of non-significance.
Comparison with the NJPPP study is relevant but lacks critique of differences in test protocols and participant characteristics; elaborate on methodological contrasts.
The Evaluation header is functional but could be more informative by summarizing the main evaluation themes (e.g., reliability, validity) at its outset.
The suggestion to wait for athletes to calm down addresses anxiety but needs a protocol description (e.g., minimum rest time) and rationale for chosen duration.
Conducting the test outside the competitive environment may control anxiety but introduce motivation differences; pilot this approach to assess trade-offs.
Noting sample homogeneity within one club highlights a limitation; strengthen improvement by planning recruitment across multiple teams.
Considering heart rate to define effort is insightful, but you must specify monitoring protocols (e.g., device type, sampling rate) to standardize measurements.

The processing of data relevant to addressing the research question is carried out but with some significant omissions, inaccuracies or inconsistencies.

The recording and processing of data shows evidence of a consideration of uncertainties but with some significant omissions or inaccuracies.
The processing of data relevant to addressing the research question is carried out appropriately and accurately.

The communication of the recording and processing of the data is both clear and precise.
